title: How Cloudflare Solved a Congestion Bug in quiche
gist: Cloudflare engineers discovered and fixed a bug in their Rust CUBIC congestion controller implementation within their open-source QUIC library quiche, where idle time was measured incorrectly causing a never-ending recovery loop under heavy packet loss. The root cause traced to a Linux kernel change for TCP, and the fix was a near-one-line change to also measure idle time from the last ACK received rather than only from the last sent data.

insights.claim: Cloudflare's Rust CUBIC congestion controller implementation in quiche entered a never-ending recovery loop when heavy packet loss occurred during connection slow start, causing ~60% of test runs to time out in a 10-second window for a 10MB download.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Real-world protocol implementation bugs in widely-deployed infrastructure can silently degrade throughput for a large fraction of connections without obvious failure signals.
insights.quote: in multiple 100-run executions, around 60% of them could not complete before the 10-second timeout

insights.claim: Cloudflare's quiche QUIC library sits in the critical path for a significant part of the traffic Cloudflare serves, making correctness of its congestion controller high-stakes.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Bugs in foundational network protocol libraries at Cloudflare's scale have outsized impact on global internet traffic performance.
insights.quote: their open-source Quick UDP Internet Connections (QUIC) implementation, quiche, which sits in the critical path for a significant part of the traffic they serve

insights.claim: The CUBIC bug in quiche was traced back to a change in the Linux kernel that was originally intended to fix a TCP issue, illustrating how kernel-level decisions propagate into application-layer protocol bugs.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Cross-layer dependencies mean that OS kernel patches can silently introduce bugs in user-space protocol implementations, even when the kernel change itself was correct.
insights.quote: The team traced the issue back to a change in the Linux kernel that aimed at fixing a real problem in TCP.

insights.claim: Replacing CUBIC with Reno in quiche resulted in 100% test pass rate under the same 30% packet loss scenario, scoping the congestion loop bug strictly to the CUBIC implementation.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Algorithmic choice in congestion controllers is a meaningful reliability variable; CUBIC's more complex window calculation creates edge cases Reno's simpler linear model avoids.
insights.quote: The simulated Reno test passed 100% of the time, scoping the problem to CUBIC only.

insights.claim: CUBIC's congestion window experienced ~999 state transitions between congestion avoidance and recovery in approximately 6.7 seconds — roughly one transition per 14ms — suspiciously close to the configured 10ms RTT, indicating pathological timer interaction.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Protocol implementation bugs triggered by RTT-aligned timers can produce very high-frequency but silent thrashing that is almost impossible to detect without deep instrumentation.
insights.quote: 999 changes in approximately 6.7 seconds: one transition per ~14ms, which the team deemed suspiciously close to the configured connection's RTT of 10ms

themes.theme: Root Cause: Idle Time Measurement in CUBIC

insights.claim: The CUBIC bug's root cause was that idle time was measured only from the last sent data packet, not from the last ACK received, causing an idle period optimization to become a self-fulfilling recovery loop at minimum congestion window sizes.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Subtle measurement reference point errors in congestion controllers can trigger catastrophic state machine loops under realistic loss conditions.
insights.quote: the way CUBIC calculates idle time caused the implementation to enter a never-ending recovery loop

insights.claim: At the minimum congestion window of two packets, CUBIC's idle period optimisation became a self-fulfilling prophecy: keeping the application in recovery state with an aggressively future recovery time that blocked congestion window growth.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Minimum window boundary conditions are a known fragile zone in congestion control design; engineers should prioritize testing at boundary congestion window sizes.
insights.quote: At a minimum congestion window of two packets, the idle period optimisation, the authors say, becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy.

insights.claim: Cloudflare resolved the CUBIC congestion loop bug by measuring idle time from both the last sent data AND the last ACK received, a near-one-line fix that restored 100% test pass rates.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: High-impact network bugs at scale can have disproportionately simple fixes once the root cause is precisely identified, underscoring the value of deep instrumentation during debugging.
insights.quote: It has a happy ending: an elegant (near-)one-line fix that broke the cycle

insights.claim: The fix required detailed instrumentation of CUBIC's internal state transitions to detect the recovery loop pattern, demonstrating that systematic observability tooling is necessary for diagnosing congestion controller bugs under noisy network conditions.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Investing in protocol-level observability and reproducible test harnesses is essential for diagnosing subtle congestion control regressions in production network systems.

themes.theme: Reproducible Testing Methodology for Protocol Bugs

insights.claim: Cloudflare used a localhost HTTP/3 simulation with 30% randomised packet loss injected in the first two seconds, configured RTT of 10ms, and a 10-second timeout to reliably reproduce a failure that was only intermittently visible in production integration tests.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Controlled localhost simulations with injected loss and explicit RTT configuration are a practical methodology for isolating congestion control bugs that are hard to reproduce in distributed environments.

insights.claim: Cloudflare engineers ran 100-iteration execution batches of their simulated test to quantify failure rate statistically (60% failure), providing a reproducible benchmark baseline for validating the fix.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Statistical validation across many runs (not single-run pass/fail) is necessary for debugging probabilistic network failures like randomised packet loss scenarios.
insights.quote: in multiple 100-run executions, around 60% of them could not complete before the 10-second timeout

insights.claim: Community observers noted similar bugs at the intersection of Linux kernel power management (C-state transitions) and protocol-level retransmit behavior, suggesting that kernel-induced latency variance is a broader class of hard-to-diagnose protocol bug.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Kernel-protocol interaction bugs are a recurring class of infrastructure failure that require cross-layer debugging skills, pointing to a talent and tooling gap for distributed systems engineers.
insights.quote: We hit something eerily similar with high-frequency timer-dependent workloads where C-state transitions were adding unpredictable latency spikes.

themes.theme: QUIC and HTTP/3 Infrastructure Maturity at Cloudflare

insights.claim: Cloudflare's quiche is an open-source Rust implementation of QUIC used in their ingress proxy, indicating Cloudflare has deployed QUIC/HTTP/3 at production scale in their own infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Cloudflare's use of a home-grown Rust QUIC stack in production signals the maturity of QUIC adoption among hyperscale CDN/proxy operators, with downstream implications for HTTP/3 deployment rates industry-wide.

insights.claim: Cloudflare's integration test pipeline for quiche exercises CUBIC under heavy-loss scenarios, suggesting their CI infrastructure includes realistic network degradation simulation as a quality gate.
insights.speaker: Gianmarco Nalin
insights.so_what: Having network-loss simulation in CI pipelines is a best practice now adopted by at least one major CDN for protocol library testing.
insights.quote: their investigation started from reports of unexpected failures in their ingress proxy integration test pipeline
